_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning to use our knowledge of sounds, word parts and vocabulary to read and spell words that share common letter patterns but have different pronunciations or meanings.

_source.w: We are learning to use our knowledge of sounds, word parts and vocabulary to read and spell words that share common letter patterns but have different pronunciations or meanings.
y: English has many words that look similar but sound different — understanding this prevents reading errors.
Combining phonological, morphological and vocabulary knowledge gives us multiple strategies for tricky words.
This flexibility is what distinguishes a skilled reader from a developing one.
eg: We compare words like 'cough', 'through' and 'though' — same letter pattern, different sounds.
We use context to determine the correct pronunciation of words that can be said two ways — like 'read' (past or present).
We identify word families where the base word helps us read and spell related words.
We practise reading homophones and homographs accurately using context.
We use morphological knowledge to decode unfamiliar words by identifying their parts.
We discuss how word origins explain why some English words have unexpected spellings.
